skill.name: documentation
skill.description: Documentation specialist for API references, OpenAPI specs, technical guides, design docs, migration guides, ADRs, and changelogs.
skill.summary: Create structured technical documentation for APIs, releases, architecture, and product changes.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples and templates. No executable scripts, prompt injection attempts, credential exfiltration logic, or malicious intent were found in the reviewed skill files. Publish with a low-risk note because examples include shell commands, curl requests, URLs, and API key placeholders that users should review before running or publishing.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution hits are documentation examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported backtick and command patterns appear inside Markdown code fences and usage examples for OpenAPI generation, SDK installation, curl examples, and prompts. They are not executable skill code, but users should review commands before running generated instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are visible in Markdown documentation and fenced examples, not in runnable source files. The skill package contains reference documents and templates rather than scripts that execute commands.

low_findings.title: Network and URL hits are API documentation placeholders
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Hardcoded URLs and HTTP libraries are used to demonstrate API documentation, SDK examples, README badges, and curl requests. No evidence found that the skill makes outbound requests or sends data to an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use example.com, placeholder base URLs, or badge links in documentation examples. There is no executable network client in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Credential hits are placeholder authentication examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. API key and token strings are placeholders used to teach authentication documentation patterns. No real secret values or environment-variable reads were found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential-like strings are templated values such as YOUR_API_KEY, {{API_KEY}}, and {{TOKEN}}. They are clearly examples for generated documentation, not harvested credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak-crypto and sensitive-file hits are template language false positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-cryptography and Windows SAM findings are triggered by documentation words, template placeholders, or security changelog examples. No cryptographic implementation, password dumping logic, or filesystem access was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are changelog or design documentation placeholders and authentication migration text. The static categories do not match executable cryptographic or filesystem behavior in this skill.

low_findings.title: Critical dangerous-combination heuristic not confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The heuristic combined documentation examples for commands, HTTP requests, and API key placeholders into a critical alert. Semantic review found no malicious workflow, no prompt injection, and no code path that combines execution, network access, and credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The combination exists only across separate documentation examples. No executable file or instruction attempts to collect secrets and exfiltrate them.

content.value_statement: Technical teams need clear docs for APIs, releases, architecture, and migrations. This skill turns project context into structured documentation with examples, templates, and review guidance.
actual_capabilities: Drafts OpenAPI and Swagger-style API documentation structures.
Creates README files, user guides, tutorials, and developer guides.
Builds changelogs and release notes from provided change context.
Provides templates for ADRs, design specs, and migration guides.
Suggests API examples in JavaScript, Python, curl, and other languages.
Organizes documentation with headings, prerequisites, examples, and troubleshooting sections.
limitations: It does not verify that generated API examples work against a live service.
It may need repository context or commit history for accurate changelogs.
It does not replace human review for legal, security, or compliance wording.
Generated commands and examples should be checked before users run them.

use_cases.title: Document an API before launch
use_cases.description: Create endpoint references, authentication notes, schemas, examples, and versioning guidance for a new API.

use_cases.title: Prepare release communication
use_cases.description: Turn implementation notes or commits into a customer-facing changelog, release note, or product update.

use_cases.title: Standardize engineering decisions
use_cases.description: Create ADRs, design specs, and migration guides that explain technical choices and upgrade steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a README
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner documentation for a project repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a README for this project. Include purpose, installation, quick start, configuration, usage examples, troubleshooting, and license sections.

prompt_templates.title: Document API endpoints
prompt_templates.prompt: Create API documentation for these endpoints. Include authentication, request parameters, response examples, error responses, rate limits, and pagination.

prompt_templates.title: Write a migration guide
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced upgrade planning for a breaking release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a migration guide from version A to version B. Include breaking changes, prerequisites, step-by-step upgrade instructions, rollback steps, and verification checks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a documentation set
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced multi-document planning for a product launch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete documentation plan for this launch. Include API reference, getting started guide, changelog, ADRs, design spec, and reviewer checklist.

output_examples.input: A team needs documentation for a new user management API.
output_examples.output: An API reference with overview, authentication, endpoint summaries, request fields, response examples, error cases, and versioning notes.

output_examples.input: A release includes new features, fixes, and one breaking API change.
output_examples.output: A release note with sections for added features, improvements, fixes, breaking changes, migration steps, and customer impact.

output_examples.input: An engineering team chose a new database migration approach.
output_examples.output: An ADR that records context, decision, alternatives considered, consequences, rollout plan, and follow-up review items.

best_practices: Provide real project context, audience level, and documentation format before asking for output.
Review generated examples, commands, and authentication placeholders before publishing.
Keep generated docs close to the source code or release process so updates stay current.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated API examples without testing them against the actual service.
Do not include real secrets, private tokens, or customer data in prompts or examples.
Do not use one generic document for different audiences with different needs.

faq.question: Can this skill create OpenAPI documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft OpenAPI-style structures, endpoint references, schemas, examples, authentication notes, and versioning guidance.

faq.question: Can it generate changelogs from commits?
faq.answer: Yes, when commit history or change notes are available. Review the result before publishing to customers.

faq.question: Does it run API requests or validate live endpoints?
faq.answer: No. It writes documentation examples and guidance. Live endpoint testing requires separate tools or manual verification.

faq.question: Is it useful for non-API documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports READMEs, user guides, design specs, ADRs, migration guides, tutorials, and release notes.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing generated docs?
faq.answer: Check technical accuracy, command safety, authentication placeholders, product names, version numbers, and any compliance-sensitive text.
